title: Mathematical models of human memory
speaker: Michail Tsodyks
affiliation: C.V. Starr Professor, School of Natural Sciences
abstract: Human memory is a multi-staged phenomenon of extreme complexity, which results in highly unpredictable behavior in real-life situations. Psychologists developed classical paradigms for studying memory in the lab, which produce easily quantifiable measures of performance at the cost of using artificial content, such as lists of randomly assembled words. I will introduce a set of simple mathematical models describing how information is maintained and recalled in these experiments.

proof_stages.label: Laboratory decomposition of memory
proof_stages.summary: The talk reduces complex real-life remembering to acquisition, maintenance, and recall, and introduces word-list recognition and recall paradigms as ways to quantify memory and recall separately.

proof_stages.label: Empirical recall-memory gap
proof_stages.summary: Classical list-learning data show that people recall far fewer items than they remember, with recall and memory curves exhibiting apparent power-law behavior as list length grows.

proof_stages.label: Sparse overlap dynamics for recall
proof_stages.summary: Each remembered item is modeled as a random sparse binary neuronal pattern, and free recall is modeled as a deterministic walk that moves to the allowed item with maximal overlap with the current pattern.

proof_stages.label: Recall-capacity asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: The average length of the deterministic recall trajectory is identified with recall capacity and argued to scale as a power law K(f)m^{alpha(f)}, with a more precise square-root law in the very sparse random-symmetric-matrix limit.

proof_stages.label: Empirical validation of recall model
proof_stages.summary: Matched recognition and recall experiments estimate m and r under the same conditions and find means and distributions close to the sparse-limit prediction, while cycle and reminder experiments test the deterministic trapping mechanism.

proof_stages.label: Interference-dominance model for maintenance
proof_stages.summary: To model maintenance after acquisition, forgetting is proposed to result from interference: a new memory carrying one or more values erases older memories dominated by it in all relevant value dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Retention asymptotics and dimension fit
proof_stages.summary: Iterative and asymptotic calculations give retention probabilities with power-law decay and logarithmic corrections, and recognition-during-list experiments fit the observed retention curve best at about five value dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Poset linear-extension prediction
proof_stages.summary: The multidimensional erasure relation is recast as a partial order, yielding the prediction that any finite set of memories can be reordered by a linear extension, and suggesting iterative experiments to find such low-forgetting orders.

examples.name: Remembering the lecture
examples.note: The speaker uses the audience's later attempt to recount the talk as a concrete illustration of acquisition, maintenance, and recall as separable stages.

examples.name: Finite overlap transition cartoons
examples.note: Small overlap diagrams and a finite red/black transition graph make the maximal-overlap rule visible and show how deterministic recall can enter a cycle while leaving some items unrecalled.
examples.engagement_reason: the finite overlap cartoons are traced step by step, choosing largest and second-largest overlaps and following the resulting transitions into loops

passes.reason: The finite overlap diagrams are ordinary toy instances illustrating the deterministic transition rule and its cycles.
A finite greedy-overlap transition instance is used to show recall can get trapped in a cycle and leave an item unrecalled.
The finite overlap cartoons illustrate generic eventual-cycle behavior of the recall rule, not a refutation.

ordinary finite overlap/transition configuration used to show deterministic recall can get trapped in a cycle and miss an item

The finite overlap cartoon shows deterministic largest-overlap recall getting trapped in a cycle and failing to reach a stored item.

ordinary finite overlap-transition toy used to visualize the deterministic recall rule and its eventual cycle

finite toy overlap-transition instances illustrate the deterministic nearest-overlap recall rule and its cycles, without being structurally special

examples.name: Random mapping and birthday paradox
examples.note: In response to a question about the square-root law, the speaker compares the recall walk to a completely random directed map, where the birthday-paradox calculation explains why trajectory length is of order sqrt(m).
examples.engagement_reason: the random-map/birthday-paradox model is simplified to an asymmetric random matrix and used to obtain the square-root trajectory-length formula

passes.reason: The random mapping/birthday-paradox model is a standard random finite-map heuristic used to explain square-root collision scaling.
The random self-map/birthday-paradox model is a standard generic probabilistic baseline explaining the square-root trajectory scale.
A uniform random mapping/birthday-paradox collision model is a standard random object used as a heuristic comparison.
a standard random mapping/functional digraph is used constructively to explain the birthday-paradox square-root collision scale
A standard random mapping model is used positively to explain the birthday-paradox square-root scaling.
standard random self-map/birthday-paradox model used heuristically to explain square-root trajectory length
A standard random mapping/birthday-paradox model is used to explain the square-root scaling heuristically.
The random mapping/birthday-paradox model is a standard random functional graph used heuristically to explain square-root scaling.
a standard random mapping/birthday-paradox model used as a representative simplification giving square-root collision scaling
generic random self-map used to explain birthday-paradox square-root scaling, not as an adversarial case

examples.name: One-dimensional value-bar erasure picture
examples.note: A sequence of bars with different heights illustrates the rule that a newly acquired higher-valued memory erases all older lower-valued memories, leading to the simple 1/t survival heuristic.
examples.engagement_reason: the one-dimensional value-bar model is run through explicit erasures and used to compute survival probability as 1/t and discuss logarithmic accumulation

passes.reason: The one-dimensional value model is the minimal dominance case and is used to show the naive erasure rule gives implausibly logarithmic total memory.
The one-dimensional value model is the minimal boundary case and is used to show the naive erasure model gives an impossible logarithmic memory total.
The one-value model is the minimal one-dimensional boundary case and its logarithmic capacity is used to rule it out.
the one-dimensional scalar-value erasure model is the minimal collapsed dominance case and is used to show the one-value hypothesis gives implausibly logarithmic total memory
The one-dimensional value model is the collapsed base case and is rejected because it predicts only logarithmically many retained memories.
one-dimensional minimal erasure model is used as a failed toy case whose logarithmic memory total is unrealistic
The one-dimensional scalar-value erasure model is the minimal boundary case and is rejected because its logarithmic memory growth is unrealistic.
The one-dimensional scalar-value erasure model is the minimal dominance case and is used to show the naive model gives unrealistically slow logarithmic memory accumulation.
the one-dimensional value model is a minimal/base-case erasure process and is shown to fail by predicting unrealistically few retained memories
collapsed one-dimensional erasure base case used to show the model gives unrealistically small logarithmic long-term memory
The one-dimensional value model is the minimal boundary case and is used to show the naive erasure model gives an unrealistic logarithmic memory count.
the one-dimensional value-bar erasure model is the minimal collapsed case and is used to show the naive model gives an untenable logarithmic memory count

examples.name: Finite dominance-poset rearrangement
examples.note: A small finite set of multidimensional memories illustrates incomparability, dominance erasure, and the linear-extension idea that the same memories can be ordered so that none erases another.
examples.engagement_reason: the finite dominance poset is concretely rearranged via a linear-extension idea and an iterative procedure moving remembered items to the front

passes.reason: The finite poset linear-extension rearrangement is an optimal ordering that avoids all erasures and retains every item.
The linear-extension rearrangement is an optimal order of a finite dominance poset that retains all items rather than refuting a claim.
A linear extension is an optimal zero-erasure ordering retaining all elements of the finite dominance poset.
a linear extension of the finite dominance poset is a no-erasure ordering achieving maximal possible retention, used as a constructive prediction
A linear-extension rearrangement is an optimal order in which the finite dominance poset retains all memories.
linear-extension rearrangement of a finite dominance poset achieves the maximal outcome of no erasures
A linear extension of a finite dominance poset is an optimal ordering that retains all memories rather than an adversarial example.
A linear extension of a finite dominance poset is an optimal rearrangement retaining all items with no erasures.
a linear-extension rearrangement of a finite dominance poset is used positively as an order achieving maximal retention with no erasures
linear extension of a finite dominance poset chosen to retain all items, giving an optimal no-erasure order
A linear extension of a finite dominance poset is an ordering that optimally prevents any erasures, used as a positive construction.
